{"covered_dims": {"R1.6": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.4, "R1.3": 0.3}}

Polished the section by cutting hedges, removing repetition, and tightening prose; flagged two substantive issues at the end.

---

**Revised text:**

We evaluate our approach on five sentiment classification benchmarks: SST-2, IMDB, Yelp, Amazon, and Twitter. Across all five, our model matches or modestly exceeds previously reported baselines.

Table X summarizes the main results. On SST-2, we reach 92.4% accuracy versus 91.8% for BERT-base; on IMDB, 93.1% versus 92.6%; and on Yelp, 96.2% versus 95.7%. The same pattern holds on Amazon and Twitter, suggesting that the gains generalize across domains and registers.

Performance is stable across random seeds [report std. or range here].

Our method may also reduce inference cost, but we have not benchmarked this across hardware configurations and treat the efficiency claim as preliminary.

---

**Notes on substance (beyond prose):**

1. The three SST-2/IMDB/Yelp gains (0.5–0.6 points) are described as "modest improvements," but with no variance reported it is unclear whether they exceed seed-level noise. You assert seed stability later — consider moving that evidence forward and quantifying it (mean ± std, or significance test), otherwise the headline claim is undersupported.
2. The Amazon and Twitter numbers are referenced but never given. Either report them or drop the appeal to them; as written, "consistent with the pattern" is unverifiable.
3. "Comparably to or somewhat better than" undercuts the contribution. If the numbers are real wins, state them plainly; if they are within noise, say so. Avoid splitting the difference.
4. Consider adding the comparison set (is BERT-base the only baseline? What about RoBERTa, or task-specific SOTA?) — a single baseline on five datasets is thin.

No external facts asserted; recommendations 1–4 are editorial judgments contingent on data you have but did not include in the excerpt.
